turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: Justice Sotomayor has the opinion of the Court this morning in Case 22-1079, Truck Insurance Exchange versus Kaiser Gypsum Company.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Truck Insurance Exchange is the primary insurer for companies that manufactured and sold products containing asbestos. Those companies filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy after facing thousands of asbestos-related lawsuits. Truck is obligated to pay up to $500,000 per asbestos claim, covered under its insurance contracts with the companies. Truck sought to object to the company’s bankruptcy reorganization plan primarily because the plan lacked disclosure requirements that Truck thinks could save it from paying millions of dollars in fraudulent claims. Section 1109(b) of the Bankruptcy Code allows any "party in interest" to raise and be heard on any issue in a Chapter 11 bankruptcy. The question in this case is whether an insurer, such as Truck, with financial responsibility for a bankruptcy claim is a party in interest under this provision. The Court of Appeals concluded that Truck was not a party in interest because the reorganization plan was insurance neutral; that is, the plan neither increased Truck’s pre-petition obligations nor impaired its rights under the insurance contracts. We disagree. The insurance neutrality doctrine conflates the merits of an insurer’s objection with the threshold 1109(b)’s question of who qualifies as a party in interest. Section 1109(b) asks whether the reorganization proceedings might directly affect the prospective party, not how a particular reorganization plan affects that party. We conclude that insurers such as Truck, with financial responsibility for bankruptcy claims, are sufficiently concerned with or affected by the proceedings to be a party in interest that can raise objections to a reorganization plan. Bankruptcy reorganization proceedings can affect an insurer’s interest in myriad ways. Truck, for example, will have to pay the vast majority of the company’s asbestos liability, and the potential financial harm for millions of dollars in fraudulent claims gives Truck an interest in the bankruptcy proceedings. Section 1109(b) grants insurers neither a vote nor a veto in reorganization proceedings. It simply provides them a voice. For these and other reasons set forth in the opinion, the judgment of the Court of Appeals is reversed and the case is remanded for further proceedings consistent with this opinion. The decision of the Court is unanimous. Justice Alito took no part in the consideration or decision of this case.
